scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: Covers all rubric-required pieces — chat loop (chat_completion.rb:73/:297), function DSL (function_dispatch.rb:46), schema conversion (function_tool_adapter.rb:50), MCP integration (mcp/, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb, tool.rb), and both message adapters — with line numbers, so an agent can enter at any of those points with only small follow-up reads. Minor gap: the MCP transport clients' roles are named but their relationship to the dispatch path is described only in a single clause.

specificity.rationale: Every abstraction is anchored to a file and module name (ChatCompletion, FunctionDispatch, FunctionToolAdapter, MessageAdapters::Base, MultimodalContentAdapter) and four key entry points carry explicit line numbers; nothing is left as 'the chat code' or 'the function code'.

justification.rationale: The answer states 'everything ships as ActiveSupport concerns you mix into a host class' and notes MCP tools flow through 'the same function/dispatch path,' touching the composable-behavior story; but it does not explain WHY the mixin design was chosen over inheritance (a-la-carte inclusion so any Ruby class gains LLM capabilities without a mandatory base class), leaving the design rationale implicit.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents all relationships as established fact with no hedges — it does not flag where an agent should verify (e.g., whether ChatCompletion and FunctionDispatch must be co-included in a specific order, or which concerns are optional), so an agent acting on it might miss compositional constraints.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces all nine layers of the round-trip with file:line throughout — entry at chat_completion.rb:73, request/normalize at :147/:340, detect at :160, max_tool_calls guard at :165-177, dispatch loop at :180-187, execute+record via function_dispatch.rb:80-101, recurse at :195-209, stop_tool_calls_and_respond at function_dispatch.rb:122-124, and terminal text at :221-235. An agent could pinpoint any layer to change with only small targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every step names the exact method, line range, and variable (e.g., `tool_call_count` at :162, `dispatch_tool_function` at :186, `instance_exec` at function_dispatch.rb:75, `stop_tool_calls_and_respond!` at :122-124) rather than vague phrases like 'the chat loop'; specificity is consistently high across all nine steps.

justification.rationale: The answer describes WHAT happens at each step but rarely explains WHY — it doesn't explain why two transcript entries are needed (assistant + tool to maintain the conversation structure LLMs expect), why the loop recurses rather than iterates, or why normalization back to an OpenAI-shaped hash is required; the design rationale is largely implicit rather than stated.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents all claims with full confidence and no verification flags — it doesn't flag the max_tool_calls cutoff branch, the multiple-tool-calls-in-one-response path, or the stop_tool_calls_and_respond shortcut as 'confirm before acting', so an agent following this map has no explicit signals about where the tracing might be wrong or incomplete.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full pipeline with file:line at every joint: DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46, storage via attr_reader at :27, tools method at :126-130, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57, create_tool_from_function at :6-48, filtering at chat_completion.rb:91-97 and filtered_tools at :303-313, and MCP filtering at mcp.rb:85-93 — enough for an agent to add a new declared capability with only targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every abstraction is named exactly (FunctionDispatch.function, @functions, tools, FunctionToolAdapter, available_tools, filtered_tools, UndeclaredToolError) and bound to file:line; no vague references like 'the function code' appear.

justification.rationale: The answer names the class-level DSL and that it allows the mixin to read declarations off the class, but never states WHY that design choice exists — i.e., so a host class declares tools declaratively at the class level and the mixin adds dispatch behavior a la carte; the 'two-layer' split (params[:tools] vs ruby_llm chat tools) is identified but not explained mechanically.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer explicitly flags the critical mismatch — 'convert_tools_for_ruby_llm ignores the filtered list… filtering currently only narrows params[:tools], not the tools actually attached to the ruby_llm chat' — which is exactly the kind of gotcha an agent must verify before changing the contract; minor parameter-schema edge cases (additionalProperties, strict) are also surfaced.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three adapters are named with exact files and line numbers (transcript_adapter.rb:14-23, message_adapters/base.rb:18-47, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20-61) and their insertion points in chat_completion.rb are precise (:133, :139, :270-272, :358); the ruby_llm_request replay at :349-372 rounds out the flow so the agent can pick any entry point with no exploratory lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every adapter is tied to a concrete file and method-level line ranges (add_message_from_hash :63-80, normalize_message_format :99-119, translatable? :42-46, attachment_source :51-61) rather than vague labels; specificity is as high as a static read can provide.

justification.rationale: The answer describes WHAT each adapter does (legacy-shape mapping, cache-control wrapping, image detection) but states the purpose implicitly; it never explicitly says why the pipeline is layered (provider-shape normalization, multimodal normalization as separate concerns) so the agent must infer the design rationale rather than read it.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditions are noted (cache_at gate, user-only multimodal, translatable? check) but the answer never flags what to verify or where it may be incomplete (e.g., whether other providers trigger different base.rb branches, or whether tool/assistant paths through ruby_llm_request have their own adapter logic); the agent gets no explicit cues about where to double-check.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: All three required pieces appear with file:line: Raix::MCP::Tool at mcp/tool.rb:11, both transport clients at sse_client.rb and stdio_client.rb with specific line numbers, and mcp.rb:96 as the exact join point where FunctionDispatch is included. The agent can go straight to mcp.rb:99-158 to see how registration works and to chat_completion.rb:184,186,297 to see the shared dispatch path.

specificity.rationale: Every claim is anchored to a file and line: `function(local_name)` at mcp.rb:108, schema overwrite at :109-113, `@tool_schemas` at :115-117, proxy method at :120-157, transcript append at :131-152. Nothing is described as 'the MCP code' — each piece has a precise address.

justification.rationale: The answer explicitly states WHY the design works: 'Ensures FunctionDispatch is included (mcp.rb:96) — so MCP tools land in the same self.class.functions registry' and 'Because MCP tools register as ordinary functions, they flow through Steps 2–4 identically.' The one-calling-path rationale is clear, though the design motivation (why this is preferable to a separate path) is implicit rather than stated.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer describes the external-path differences (name mangling via unique_key, schema source from input_schema, coerce_arguments step, call_tool transport) but frames them as settled facts rather than flagging them as spots where an agent should verify before acting. There is no explicit 'confirm before relying on' or acknowledgment that transport behavior or schema coercion could have edge cases.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer enumerates all six areas the rubric names — chat loop, declaration+schema, conversation adapters, MCP external tools, and the spec matrix — each with specific file:line ranges; the VCR cassettes catch is a genuine bonus site. The one gap is that the mixin-composition dynamic (these paths are only reachable when modules are included into a host class) is not named, so the agent still needs to confirm that architectural constraint before acting.

specificity.rationale: Every site carries a concrete file name and line range (e.g., `chat_completion.rb :160-161`, `function_dispatch.rb :46-65`, `mcp/sse_client.rb:32,47,86,80`); no site is left at the level of 'the tool code' or 'the tests'.

justification.rationale: Section headers ('Chat loop / dispatch', 'Declaration → schema', 'Conversation adapters', 'External tools') implicitly assign a role to each area, but the answer never states in prose WHY a site is bound to the contract (e.g., why transcript_adapter.rb must change when the result message shape changes, or why the VCR cassettes encode the wire contract); the agent can infer from the groupings but must reconstruct the causal logic itself.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the list as complete and authoritative; it does not flag that mixin paths are only live when included in a host class, does not note variability across MCP transports, and does not warn that static reading may miss dynamic dispatch sites — exactly the gaps the rubric asks the answer to flag.

map_quality.rationale: All four areas are mapped with file:line — chat loop (`:91-97`, `:160-187`, `:297-313`, `:360-385`, `:424-439`), declaration+schema (function_dispatch.rb:46-130, function_tool_adapter.rb:6-57), adapters (transcript_adapter.rb:82-119, base.rb:17-47, multimodal:20-46), and external path (mcp.rb:99-250, mcp/tool.rb:11-65, sse/stdio clients) — plus specs to update/add and VCR cassettes; the JSON block doubles as a machine-readable summary, leaving an agent ready to open the PR.

specificity.rationale: Every change point is named by file and line range with no vague phrases; even indirect dependencies like `ruby_llm_request_metadata_spec.rb` and `default_temperature_spec.rb` are called out individually alongside `spec/vcr/Raix_FunctionDispatch/*.yml`.

justification.rationale: Inline notes explain some ownership ("override point for new dispatch semantics", "keep :131-152 in lockstep with function_dispatch.rb:80-101", "only if new content kinds added"), but the map does not consistently articulate *why* each file is a required change site vs. a contingent one, leaving the agent to infer from context for several entries.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags the `convert_tools_for_ruby_llm` gap, the conditional multimodal adapter, the lockstep MCP requirement, and VCR regeneration as conditional; it does not explicitly mark the loop re-entry recursion or the schema format as points to verify before committing, which are the trickiest parts of the contract change.
